provenance.source_corpus: A Century of Elk Restoration in Eastern North America

source_spans.text: The reduction in elk populations and range has been primarily attributed to overexploitation and habitat loss (O’Gara & Dundas 2002).
The reduction in elk populations and range has been primarily attributed to overexploitation and habitat loss (O’Gara & Dundas 2002).
The reduction in elk populations and range has been primarily attributed to overexploitation and habitat loss (O’Gara & Dundas 2002).
Hunting seasons have been opened in several regions and contribute to population control and potentially account for slower rates of increase (i.e. Minnesota, United States)
Hunting seasons have been opened in several regions and contribute to population control and potentially account for slower rates of increase (i.e. Minnesota, United States)
Griffith et al. (1989) found that larger founding populations were more successful for large mammal reintroductions
Griffith et al. (1989) found that larger founding populations were more successful for large mammal reintroductions
Small founder populations may affect more than population growth rates, as they often result in reduced genetic variability due to the founder effect and genetic drift (Conard et al. 2010).
Small founder populations may affect more than population growth rates, as they often result in reduced genetic variability due to the founder effect and genetic drift (Conard et al. 2010).

Post-release dispersal can be reduced by holding elk for longer periods of time prior to release to acclimate them to release sites. This has been shown to result in greater post-release site fidelity in Ontario elk (Ryckman et al. 2010).

Post-release dispersal can be reduced by holding elk for longer periods of time prior to release to acclimate them to release sites. This has been shown to result in greater post-release site fidelity in Ontario elk (Ryckman et al. 2010).

Predation was found to be a limiting factor in the Burwash and LOW populations of Ontario, accounting for 40.6 and 17.6% of the initial mortality, respectively. These populations have low or negative growth rates, suggesting that predation may be a strong factor influencing population growth.

Predation was found to be a limiting factor in the Burwash and LOW populations of Ontario, accounting for 40.6 and 17.6% of the initial mortality, respectively. These populations have low or negative growth rates, suggesting that predation may be a strong factor influencing population growth.
Vehicle and train collisions accounted for 2.9–16% of post-release elk mortality in eastern North America (Table 2).
Vehicle and train collisions accounted for 2.9–16% of post-release elk mortality in eastern North America (Table 2).
Mortality caused by meningeal worm (Parelaphostrongylus tenuis) accounted for 45.3% of reported deaths in Kentucky, 8.1% in Tennessee, and 10% in Arkansas during the pre-acclimation period (Table 2).

nodes.name: High total number of elk released

source_spans.text: Kentucky’s high rate of increase, with a current population of 10,000 elk, appears to be related to the high total number of elk released (Table 1)

nodes.name: Areas dominated by a single cover type

source_spans.text: Areas dominated by a single cover type should be avoided, as they will likely result in lower reintroduction success.
Areas dominated by a single cover type should be avoided, as they will likely result in lower reintroduction success.
